1: YHWH spoke to Moses, saying — Leviticus 6 opens a new section with a second revelation formula; in the Hebrew Bible the chapter division begins what rabbinic tradition calls the 'priestly torah' (torat ha-kohanim) section, containing detailed instructions for the priests' execution of each sacrifice type. Vv.1-7 (Hebrews 5:20-26) address the asham for interpersonal fraud before returning to the altar-service rubrics.
2: If anyone sins and commits a breach of faith against YHWH by deceiving his neighbor in a matter of deposit or security (piqqadon o vitesumeth yad), or through robbery, or if he has oppressed his neighbor — the ma'al (breach of faith) category expands here to cover interpersonal wrongs that have a perjury dimension: false denial of a deposit, fraud regarding a pledge, theft, oppression, and false oath. The asham's trigger is the false oath by which the perpetrator invoked YHWH's name to conceal the wrongdoing (Leviticus 6:3), which transforms an interpersonal tort into a sacred violation.
3: Or has found something lost and lied about it, swearing falsely (nishba al-shaqer) — the false oath is the hinge that connects the interpersonal wrong to YHWH: by swearing falsely in YHWH's name, the offender has made YHWH complicit in the lie, creating a sacrilege dimension that triggers the asham mechanism alongside any civil restitution. The Decalogue's prohibition of false swearing (Exodus 20:7) is enacted here through the asham procedure.
4: If he has sinned and has realized his guilt, he shall restore what he took by robbery or what he got by oppression or the deposit that was committed to him or the lost thing that he found — the sequence of restoration (hashiv) requires returning the specific item or its equivalent; the civil and cultic restoration are inseparable. The rabbinic concept that the stolen item must be returned before the sacrifice is accepted (b. Bava Kamma 94b) derives from this structure.
5: Or anything about which he has sworn falsely; he shall restore it in full (berosho) and shall add a fifth to it (chomishitav yosif alav), and give it to him to whom it belongs on the day he realizes his guilt — the chomesh (one-fifth surcharge) applies to interpersonal restitution as to sacrilege against holy things (Leviticus 5:16); the surcharge must be delivered on the same day as the acknowledgment of guilt, preventing delay. The precision about timing (day of guilt-realization) reflects the system's concern with the quality of repentance, not merely formal legal compliance.
6: And he shall bring to the priest as his compensation to YHWH a ram without blemish from the flock, or its equivalent, for a guilt offering — the same ram requirement as Leviticus 5:15,18; the asham for interpersonal fraud uses the identical animal as the asham for sacrilege against holy things, confirming that false swearing in YHWH's name elevates interpersonal tort into the sacred category. The civil restitution (Leviticus 6:4-5) and the cultic offering (Leviticus 6:6-7) are both required; neither alone is sufficient.
7: And the priest shall make atonement for him before YHWH, and he shall be forgiven for any of the things that one may do and thereby become guilty — the comprehensive forgiveness formula: whatever the specific fraud category (robbery, false deposit denial, lost-property fraud, false oath), the atonement mechanism is the same. The breadth of the formula reflects the chatat/asham system's capacity to address the full range of unwitting and acknowledged violations.
8: YHWH spoke to Moses, saying — the third revelation formula in Leviticus 5–7, introducing the priestly service rubrics for the burnt offering (Leviticus 6:9-13). These instructions address what the priests do with offerings that have been presented, whereas Leviticus 1–5 address what the offerers present.
9: Command Aaron and his sons, saying, This is the law (torah) of the burnt offering (olah). The burnt offering shall be on the hearth (al-moqedah) on the altar all night until the morning, and the fire of the altar shall be kept burning on it — the tamid (continual) principle: the olah placed on the altar in the evening must remain burning through the night. The word moqed (hearth, burning-place) describes the altar's fire-bearing surface; the all-night burning allows complete combustion of the evening sacrifice before the morning's ashes are removed.
10: And the priest shall put on his linen garment (mido bad) and put his linen undergarments on his body, and he shall take up the ashes (deshen) to which the fire has reduced the burnt offering on the altar and put them beside the altar — the priestly linen garments (Exodus 28:40-43; Leviticus 16:4) are required even for the mundane ash-removal; contact with the altar's sacred space demands vestments. The deshen (ashes/fatty remnants) of the burnt offering are treated as sacred residue; they are not simply swept away but placed respectfully beside the altar before being carried outside the camp (Leviticus 6:11).
11: Then he shall take off his garments and put on other garments and carry the ashes outside the camp to a clean place (makom tahor) — the change of garments for ash-carrying indicates a two-stage holiness gradient: the altar area requires the most sacred garments; the task of carrying ashes outside requires different (lesser?) garments. The makom tahor (clean place) is outside the camp — not the unclean zone where diseased persons and waste are disposed, but a specifically designated clean area for sacred remnants. Later rabbinic law developed the beit ha-deshen (ash-house) from this provision.
12: The fire on the altar shall be kept burning on it; it shall not go out. The priest shall burn wood on it every morning, and he shall arrange the burnt offering on it and shall burn on it the fat of the peace offerings — the fire's perpetuity (lo tikhbeh, it shall not go out) is stated three times in Leviticus 6:12-13; the eternal flame is the sign of YHWH's permanent presence and ongoing acceptance of Israel's worship. In the ancient Near East, temple fires were also carefully maintained; the Mesopotamian sacred brazier (dipāru) was kept burning in major temples, but the Levitical emphasis on the fire's divine origin (Leviticus 9:24) and permanent maintenance is more theologically charged.
13: Fire shall be kept burning on the altar continually (tamid); it shall not go out — the single word tamid (continually, perpetually) becomes the technical term for all the regular, unending acts of sanctuary service: the olat tamid (continual burnt offering, Exodus 29:42), the menorah tamid (continual lamp, Exodus 27:20), the lechem ha-panim (bread of presence, displayed continually). The perpetual altar fire is the heartbeat of the sanctuary's operation.
14: And this is the law of the grain offering (torat ha-minchah). The sons of Aaron shall offer it before YHWH in front of the altar — the grain offering rubric transitions from burnt offering to the minchah; the grain offering can be presented independently (Lev 2) or as an accompaniment to animal sacrifices (Leviticus 7:11-14; Num 15). The priestly torah for the minchah addresses the handling after presentation: the azkarah burning and the priestly portions.
15: And one of them shall take from it a handful of the fine flour of the grain offering and of its oil and all the frankincense that is on the grain offering and burn this as its memorial portion (azkaratah) on the altar, a pleasing aroma to YHWH — the kometz azkarah (handful as memorial/token) burns on the altar as the YHWH-portion; the remainder is the priestly food. The term azkarah (memorial, reminder) has generated significant debate: does it remind YHWH of the offerer, or does it serve as the offerer's memorial before YHWH? Both senses may be active.
16: And the remainder of it Aaron and his sons shall eat. It shall be eaten unleavened (matzot te'achel) in a holy place — priestly eating of the grain offering remainder is a sacred act in a sacred place; the requirement for unleavened (matzot) grain echoes the Passover prohibition on leaven and the general Levitical principle that leaven does not belong in fire-offerings to YHWH (Leviticus 2:11). The association of leaven with fermentation/corruption may underlie the restriction.
17: It shall not be baked with leaven. I have given it as their portion from my food offerings. It is most holy (qodesh qedashim hi), like the sin offering and the guilt offering — the category qodesh qedashim (most holy) restricts consumption to male priests only (cf. Leviticus 6:18) within the sanctuary precincts; this is the highest sanctity category in Levitical law, shared by the sin offering (chatat), guilt offering (asham), and grain offering (minchah).
18: Every male among the children of Aaron may eat of it, as decreed forever throughout your generations, from YHWH's food offerings. Whoever touches them shall become holy (kol asher yigga bahem yiqdash) — the touching-becomes-holy principle: contact with most-holy objects consecrates whatever (whoever) touches them. This is the opposite of impurity transmission; impurity defiles, holiness consecrates. The principle also means that unauthorized touching of most-holy objects would result in dangerous overexposure to holiness — which is why contact is restricted to authorized priests.
19: YHWH spoke to Moses, saying — a fourth revelation formula introduces the ordination grain offering (minchat chinuch, the offering for Aaron and his sons, Leviticus 6:20-23).
20: This is the offering that Aaron and his sons shall offer to YHWH on the day when he is anointed: a tenth of an ephah of fine flour as a regular grain offering, half of it in the morning and half in the evening — the high priest's daily grain offering (minchat chavitin) consists of 1/10 ephah divided into morning and evening portions, offered on a griddle, served fully to YHWH without any priestly retention. This distinguishes the high priest's offering from the lay grain offering (where only the handful burns and priests eat the rest): priestly self-offering is total, not partial.
21: It shall be made with oil on a griddle (al-machabat bashemen ta'aseh). You shall bring it well mixed, in baked pieces like a grain offering, and offer it for a pleasing aroma to YHWH — the preparation method (griddle with oil) for the daily priestly minchah parallels the lay griddle offering of Leviticus 2:5; the murcheshet (well-stirred, broken pieces) ensures even cooking. The Talmud (Menachot 78a) reconstructs the distinctive folded-and-griddle-fried preparation of the chavitin.
22: The anointed priest who is in his place among his sons shall offer it. It is a perpetual due; it shall be wholly burned to YHWH (kalil le-YHWH tiqtár) — every successor high priest continues the father's daily offering; the requirement perpetuates through the priestly genealogy. Kalil (wholly burned, complete) confirms that unlike the lay grain offering (from which priests eat a portion), the priestly grain offering is entirely consumed on the altar — a fitting symbol of priestly self-consecration.
23: Every grain offering of a priest shall be wholly burned. It shall not be eaten — the prohibition on priestly eating of their own grain offerings parallels the principle that the priests who handle sin offerings cannot eat their own sin offerings (Leviticus 6:30): the officiating priest cannot be simultaneously the beneficiary of his own sacrificial transaction.
24: YHWH spoke to Moses, saying — fifth revelation formula; the sin offering (chatat) priestly rubric (Leviticus 6:25-30) addresses consumption and handling of the most-holy sin offering blood and meat.
25: Speak to Aaron and his sons, saying, This is the law of the sin offering (torat ha-chatat). In the place where the burnt offering is killed, the sin offering shall be killed before YHWH; it is most holy — the slaughter location (north side of the altar, Leviticus 1:11) is the same for sin offering and burnt offering, preventing visual distinction between the most-solemn and the standard offerings. The most-holy status means the slaughter space itself is consecrated during the procedure.
26: The priest who offers it for sin shall eat it. In a holy place it shall be eaten, in the court of the tent of meeting (bachatzer ohel mo'ed) — the sin offering meat is priestly food, consumed within the sanctuary court; the priest who performs the atonement eats the offering, creating a physical identification between the priest and the sin he has borne for the offerer. The eating-as-bearing-sin motif (Leviticus 10:17: 'you should eat it in the holy place, since it is most holy and was given to you that you may bear the iniquity of the congregation') is explicit in later texts.
27: Whatever touches its flesh shall be holy, and when any of its blood is sprinkled on a garment, you shall wash that on which it was sprinkled in a holy place — the most-holy garment-staining rule: blood from a sin offering contaminates garments and must be laundered in the sacred precincts (not simply anywhere). The holiness-contamination of the blood (not mere impurity) reflects the concentrated sacred power of the chatat's blood, which has been applied to the altar horns or to the veil in the atoning process.
28: And the earthen vessel in which it is boiled shall be broken (keli cheres asher tevushal bo yishaver), and if it is boiled in a bronze vessel, that shall be scoured and rinsed in water — the earthenware-vs-bronze distinction for contaminated vessels: earthenware absorbs the sacred residue permanently and must be destroyed; bronze can be cleaned by scouring and rinsing. This distinction appears throughout Leviticus (Leviticus 11:33-34; Leviticus 14:5,50) as a consistent material-property principle.
29: Every male among the priests may eat of it; it is most holy — access to sin offering meat is restricted to male priests; female priests' household members and lay Israelites are excluded from this most-holy category (contrast the lesser-holy peace offering portions, which non-priestly household members may eat: Leviticus 10:14).
30: But no sin offering shall be eaten from which any blood is brought into the tent of meeting to make atonement in the Holy Place; it shall be burned up with fire — the rule that distinguishes inward-blood-manipulation (Leviticus 6:30) from outward-blood-manipulation: when the chatat blood is brought inside the tent of meeting (as in Leviticus 4:3-21 for priests and congregation), the meat is not eaten but burned outside the camp. When the blood is applied only to the outer altar (Leviticus 4:25-34 for ruler and commoner), the meat is eaten by the priests. The distinction preserves a gradient between the most intense atonement acts (requiring total burning) and the less intense ones (allowing priestly consumption).
